content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

April 7, 1977


John J. Carniato
1211 Nowell Ave., Suite 202
P.O. Box 5288
Walnut Creek, California 94596

Re: Central Contra Costa Sanitary District

Dear Mr. Carniato:

Your inquiry of March 23, 1977 has been referred to me for reply.

You represent the Central Contra Costa Sanitary District which is
a local government agency required to submit a conflict of interest code
to your Board of Supervisors. You ask two questions regarding the effect
of the Code upon various "consultants" of the district. You ask first "...
are outside independent contractors, such as the engineering firm
referred to in your letter, consultants to the District and therefore
required to file disclosure statements?"

As a general matter, consultants can be designated officials within
a conflict of interest code. More particularly it is not possible to
determine whether the consultants to which you refer are consultants
within the meaning of the various designated positions within the code.
A more fundamental problem, however, is whether the consultants to
which you refer in your letter are in fact consultants within the
meaning of the Political Reform Act. This is an extremely difficult
issue requiring an interpretation of the Commission's regulation
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(a)(2). The Commission considered this
issue in connection with "The Matter of James Botz," County Counsel,
County of Sonoma. The Commission never formally adopted the draft
opinion (attached), however, it may provide some guidance to you.

Second you ask "[c]an the District itself make the decision as to
whether disclosure statements must be filed by outside independent
contractors who perform consulting services?"

Government Code Section 87300 et seq. provides that the determination
of what positions should be designated in a code is a joint decision of the
agency adopting the Code and the code reviewing body. Ultimately, the
determination of the code reviewing body must stand unless the agency
or an individual seeks judicial review pursuant to 87308. If the Board of
Supervisors of Contra Costa County reviews codes in the manner of the


TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION 916 323-3661 • EXECUTIVE (LEGAL 323 5501 • ENFORCEMENT CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322 6441

John J. Carnia
April 7, 1977
page two


Fair Political Practices Commission, it will require certain justifications
for the designation of positions within the Conflict of Interest Code,
thus giving the district an opportunity to support its inclusion or
exclusion of consultancy positions.

     I hope this information has been of some assistance to you. If
you have further questions, please do not hesitate to call me at
(916) 322-6444.

                                                     Sincerely,


                                                     [signature]
                                                     Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
                                                     Conflicts of Interest Division

DLS:mfa
Attachment

sections.question: Are outside independent contractors, such as engineering firms, considered consultants to the District and therefore required to file disclosure statements? Can the District itself make the decision as to whether disclosure statements must be filed by outside independent contractors who perform consulting services?
sections.conclusion: Consultants can be designated officials within a conflict of interest code, but determining whether specific consultants fall within the Political Reform Act's definition requires interpretation of the relevant regulations. The determination of which positions should be designated in a conflict of interest code is a joint decision between the agency adopting the code and the code reviewing body, with the reviewing body's determination standing unless judicial review is sought.
sections.question_synthetic: The inquiry addresses whether independent contractors and engineering firms performing consulting services for a local government agency are required to file conflict of interest disclosure statements, and whether the agency itself can determine which consultant positions must file disclosures.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Whether independent contractors qualify as consultants requiring disclosure is determined through interpretation of the Political Reform Act's definition, and while the agency can propose positions for designation, the code reviewing body makes the final determination subject to judicial review.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains two distinct questions from the inquirer regarding consultant disclosure requirements. The FPPC references a draft opinion from 'The Matter of James Botz' as guidance. Letter is signed by Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief of Conflicts of Interest Division. Minor OCR issue in recipient name spelling variation ('Carniato' vs 'Carnia').

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are outside independent contractors, such as engineering firms, considered consultants to the District and therefore required to file disclosure statements? Can the District itself make the decision as to whether disclosure statements must be filed by outside independent contractors who perform consulting services?

CONCLUSION: Consultants can be designated officials within a conflict of interest code, but determining whether specific consultants fall within the Political Reform Act's definition requires interpretation of the relevant regulations. The determination of which positions should be designated in a conflict of interest code is a joint decision between the agency adopting the code and the code reviewing body, with the reviewing body's determination standing unless judicial review is sought.
embedding.first_500_words: April 7, 1977 John J. Carniato 1211 Newell Ave., Suite 202 P.O. Box 5288 Walnut Creek, California 94596 Re: Central Contra Costa Sanitary District Dear Mr. Carniato: Your inquiry of March 23, 1977 has been referred to me for reply. You represent the Central Contra Costa Sanitary District which is a local government agency required to submit a conflict of interest code to your Board of Supervisors. You ask two questions regarding the effect of the Code upon various "consultants" of the district. You ask first "...are outside independent contractors, such as the engineering firm referred to..., considered consultants to the District and therefore required to file disclosure statements?" As a general matter, consultants can be designated officials within a conflict of interest code. More particularly it is not possible to determine whether the consultants to which you refer are consultants within the meaning of the various designated positions within the code. A more fundamental problem, however, is whether the consultants to which you refer in your letter are in fact consultants within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. This is an extremely difficult issue requiring an interpretation of the Commission's regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(a)(2). The Commission considered this issue in connection with "The Matter of James Botz," County Counsel, County of Sonoma. The Commission never formally adopted the draft opinion (attached), however, it may provide some guidance to you. Second you ask "[c]an the District itself make the decision as to whether disclosure statements must be filed by outside independent contractors who perform consulting services?" Government Code Section 87300 et seq. provides that the determination of what positions should be designated in a code is a joint decision of the agency adopting the Code and the code reviewing body. Ultimately, the determination of the code reviewing body must stand unless the agency or an individual seeks judicial review pursuant to 87308. If the Board of Supervisors of Contra Costa County reviews codes in the manner of the John J. Carnia April 7, 1977 Fair Political Practices Commission, it will require certain justifications for the designation of positions within the Conflict of Interest Code, thus giving the district an opportunity to support its inclusion or exclusion of consultancy positions. I hope this information has been of some assistance to you. If you have further questions, please do not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-6444. Sincerely, [signature] Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief Conflicts of Interest Division DLS:mfa Attachment
embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to Central Contra Costa Sanitary District regarding whether outside independent contractors and engineering firms are required to file conflict of interest disclosure statements. The response explains that consultant designation is a joint decision between the agency and the code reviewing body, with ultimate authority resting with the reviewing body.
